summary: My maternal family originally came from western Germany. Specifically, they lived in the providence of Schlüchtern before immigrating to America in 1835. Unfortunately, my ancestor Mr. Yahn did not survive the journey and was buried at sea. Germany is where our story begins before we made a new life in the United States.

Schlüchtern was the providence in Germany where my family lived before coming to America. It was in this region, in the town of Vollmerz, that my ancestors lived their early lives. Their departure from Schlüchtern in 1835 marked the start of our family's American journey.

Vollmerz is the town in Schlüchtern, Germany, where my maternal ancestors lived before immigrating to the United States. It was from Vollmerz that Mr. Yahn and his family set sail in 1835. This town represents the roots of my family before they settled in Pennsylvania.

After arriving in America, my family settled in Perry Township, Lawrence County, Pennsylvania. This is where Lewis and Godfreye Yahn, the sons of Mr. Yahn, started their lives as a weaver and a farmer respectively. Perry Township became the foundation for our family's American generations.

Beaver County, Pennsylvania, is where the Swick sisters, Nancy and Lucinda, came from. They married my ancestors Lewis and Godfreye Yahn, linking two families in the area. This county played a key role in the growth of our family in Pennsylvania.

My family was deeply affected by the American Civil War, which took place in the 1860s. We fought on the side of the Union, but tragedy struck when Yahn Jr. was captured and sent to Andersonville Prison. The war brought hardship and loss to our family, reflecting the brutal reality of that era.

During the American Civil War, my family fought for the Union Army. Unfortunately, Yahn Jr. was captured and imprisoned in Andersonville, a notorious Confederate prison. Our connection to the Union Army is a significant part of our family's history and sacrifice.

Andersonville Prison was where my relative Yahn Jr. was held captive during the Civil War. It was known for its brutal conditions, with many soldiers dying from disease and malnutrition. Sadly, Yahn Jr. died there, making it a tragic chapter in our family story.

Pennsylvania has been home to my family for many generations after immigrating from Germany. We lived in places like Perry Township and Beaver County, and even up to my lifetime, we owned land near Elwood City. Pennsylvania shaped much of our early American experience.

Elwood City, about 30 miles outside of Pittsburgh, is where my family owned an acre of land during my lifetime. It represents the continuation of our roots in Pennsylvania even as later generations moved west. The land there was a tangible link to our family's past.

Pittsburgh was the nearest major city to our family land in Elwood City, Pennsylvania. Growing up, I knew it as a reference point for where we lived in relation to the larger urban area. Pittsburgh was part of the regional landscape of my family's history.

My mother, grandmother, and great aunt moved west and lived in Cleveland, Ohio for about a year in 1955. My mom's only real memory of Cleveland was playfully throwing a snowball at her mom. Cleveland was a brief but memorable stop before they settled in San Francisco.

San Francisco became home to my mother and her family after they moved west in the mid-1950s. They settled in the foggy Sunset District in late 1956, and my mother vividly remembers the 1957 earthquake there. My brothers still live in San Francisco, and it remains an important city for our family.

The Sunset District in San Francisco is where my family settled when they arrived in late 1956. Known for its foggy weather, it was the neighborhood where my mother experienced the 1957 earthquake. This district marks the beginning of our family's life on the West Coast.

The 1957 San Francisco earthquake was a major event my mother remembers from her first year living in the city. It registered a 5.7 on the Richter scale and was the strongest quake since the infamous 1906 earthquake. This event left a strong impression on our family as they settled in the Sunset District.

Though I did not live through it, the 1906 San Francisco earthquake is a significant historical event that my family references when recalling the 1957 quake. It was the last major earthquake before the one my mother experienced, highlighting the seismic history of the city we now call home.

I have lived in many places across northern California, including Arcata. It was one of the cities I called home before settling down in Oakland. Living in Arcata was part of my journey exploring different parts of the state.

Belmont, California is another city where I lived during my travels throughout northern California. It was part of my experience before I finally put down roots in Oakland. Belmont represents a chapter in my life of moving and exploring the region.

San Leandro is one of the cities in northern California where I have lived. My time there was part of a broader pattern of moving around before settling in Oakland. San Leandro contributed to my understanding of the Bay Area communities.

Concord, California was among the places I lived while moving around northern California. It was part of my journey before I established a more permanent home in Oakland. Concord helped shape my experience of the Bay Area.

For the past ten years, I have made my home in Oakland, California. I love living here because it's warmer than San Francisco but still close enough to the city for a quick commute. Oakland has become the place where I have finally put down roots after many moves around northern California.
